data.title: U.S. stocks slip as retail inflation comes in slightly cooler, earnings fail to lift sentiment
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Tuesday, with the Nasdaq Composite ((COMP:IND)) showing some strength, as investors assessed cooler-than-expected core CPI inflation and some important earnings reports.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.4% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.7%.

The December Consumer Price Index came in at +0.3% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.7% YoY vs. +2.6% consensus and +2.7% prior.

Core CPI (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.6% YoY vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior.

“Tuesday's CPI was in-line with expectations and that will likely keep the Federal Reserve on pause when it comes to interest rates for the foreseeable future… Recent positive employment data, elevated inflation, sticky price levels and political noise will keep the Fed at bay through at least the Spring,” said Skyler Weinand, CIO at Regan Capital.

“CPI is still under a cloud from the missing October number. Inflation is still relatively high, but trending downward. Consumers are still grappling with high overall price levels and sticker shock from the post-Covid jarring in housing, healthcare, education and everyday purchase prices,” Weinand added.

Also, new home sales slipped 0.1% MoM to 737K in October, vs. 716K expected, from 738K in the prior month, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Among companies reporting on Tuesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) -3.9% posted better-than-expected earnings, while Delta Air Lines (DAL) -2.4% had year-over-year revenue growth. 

Big companies reporting this week include Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Citigroup (C).

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower to 4.17%, while the U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was also 1 basis point lower to 3.53%. A 30-Year bond auction traded through.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase beat largely due to lower expenses, analysts say
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) stock slid 4.0% in Tuesday afternoon trading as the bank's Q4 earnings beat consensus, largely on lower-than-expected operating expenses, according to equity research notes. Meanwhile, 2026 guidance was in line with analysts' expectations.

Investment banking fees, which fell 5% Y/Y, were disappointing.

The bank also remains under pressure as the Trump administration moves to limit credit card interest and swipe fees, which are strong revenue generators for many banks. During JPMorgan Chase's (JPM) earnings call, Chief Financial Officer Jeremy Barnum said "everything is on the table" in its efforts to push back against the proposed 10% interest rate on credit cards.

In analyzing the company's Q4 earnings, Wolfe Research analyst Steven Chubak pointed out that, "Expenses were well controlled, as efficiency of 51.3% came in below WR est./Consensus of 52.2%/52.9%." Also, net interest income ((NII)) of $25.0B slightly topped consensus of $24.9B and Wolfe's $24.8B estimate, resulting from better loan growth and slightly better net interest margin expansion.

Evercore ISI analyst Glenn Schorr points out that "NII continues to be constrained by lower rates, and home lending was still down 4% Y/Y."

Citi analyst Keith Horowitz credits the ~$0.20 beat on preprovision net revenue to consensus on the improved expense management and the slight beat in NII, ex-markets.

Adjusted for one-time items, "core expense beat was in large part on compensation within CIB (Corporate and Investment Banking)," Horowitz wrote.

Equities trading fees also pitched in to the beat, at $2.9B, beating consensus of $2.7B and Wolfe's estimate of $2.8B, Chubak noted.

One factor to note, JPMorgan's (JPM) provision for credit losses of $4.66B, includes a $2.2B reserve taken for its upcoming purchase of the Apple credit portfolio. That reduced its GAAP net EPS by $0.60. Excluding the item, Q4 adjusted EPS was $5.23.

On the negative side of the Q4 ledger, Investment Banking fees were down 5% Y/Y, "slightly worse than conference guidance of up low-single digit; ECM (equity capital markets) was down -16% Y/Y (-21% Q/Q), Advisory was down -3% Y/Y (+12% Q/Q), while DCM (debt capital markets) was down -2% Y/Y (-24% Q/Q)," Evercore ISI's Schorr wrote in a note to clients. 

In looking ahead, analysts call the company's 2026 guidance largely in line. "We see no real surprises to 2026 guidance with $95B NII, excluding markets, consistent with previous guidance given at 3Q25 earnings, $105B ’26 expense guide maintained, and 3.4% card NCO in-line with implied December guidance range of 3.3-3.6%," Citi's Horowitz said.

data.title: SA Quant ratings: Best and worst-rated consumer staples stocks ahead of Q4 earnings
data.description:
With the fourth quarter earnings season approaching, Seeking Alpha generated a list of the highest- and lowest-rated mid- to mega-cap consumer staples stocks as per its Quant model.

Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) was the third-worst performing sector in Q4, seeing a marginal loss of 0.73%, compared to the broader market SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) (SP500) gain of 2%. 

Staples saw a defensive lag and are said to be less susceptible to economic cycles, frequently lagging in a risk-on market. The XLP Fund (XLP) holds a ~5.8% weightage in the S&P 500 index (SP500). Among the 11 sector-based ETFs, Seeking Alpha's quant ratings give the S&P consumer staples fund (XLP) the second-lowest score of 2.28, only above the real estate ETF (XLRE). 

Quant ratings

Turning to individual stocks, Seeking Alpha's quant rating system gives the following companies top ratings:

  • Ambev (ABEV) - Strong Buy with a quant rating of 4.86
  • United Natural Foods (UNFI) - Strong Buy rating of 4.73
  • Danone (DANOY) - Buy rating of 4.38
  • Jerónimo Martins (JRONY) - Buy rating 4.26
  • Heineken (HEINY) - Buy rating 4.18

Among the lowest SA quant-rated companies, all having a Strong Sell ratings are:

  • BellRing Brands (BRBR) - Strong sell with quant rating of 1.10
  • e.l.f. Beauty (ELF) - 1.22
  • Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) - 1.25
  • Primo Brands (PRMB) - 1.28
  • National Beverage (FIZZ) - 1.35

Analyst comments

Forecasting 2026, "Favor attractive & profitable categories amid a challenged Staples backdrop - Buy nicotine, energy drink, candy and beauty stocks" Goldman Sachs Research wrote in a recent note. The research house cited top ideas for 2026, including

  • Market share gainers and strong topline performers: (PM) and (MNST)
  • Where bearishness appears over-done: (PEP), (ELF), (CELH), (HSY) and (SFM)
  • Beer recovery: (STZ) and (TAP)
  • Growth-advantaged emerging market exposure: (PM), (MDLZ) and (CL)
  • Easing cost pressure: (HSY)
  • GLP-1/better-for-you positioning: (SFM)

"Although the consumer staples sector shares some similarities and is even more resilient when it comes to macroeconomic impacts, I am more bearish on this industry in the short term, and I believe that the current situation is much more complex than it appears to be." said SA contributor Kenio Fontes.

Other funds for a diversified approach to consumer staples include: Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC); iShares U.S. Consumer Staples ETF (IYK); First Trust Consumer Staples AlphaDEX Fund (FXG); Invesco Dynamic Food & Beverage ETF (PBJ); First Trust Nasdaq Food & Beverage ETF (FTXG); iShares Global Consumer Staples ETF (KXI).

data.title: Travere plummets as FDA extends Filspari review for rare kidney disorder
data.description:
• The US FDA has extended by three months to April 13 its review of an sNDA from Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) to expand the label for Filspari (sparsentan) for focal segmental glomerulosclerosis ("FSGS"), a rare kidney disorder.
  • Following a trading halt that began at 1240p ET, shares are now down ~20%. 
  • The company said that recent information provided to the agency on the drug's clinical benefit constituted a Major Amendment to the application, necessitating the additional time.
  • Travere added no additional information on safety or manufacturing is being requested.

data.title: Microcap magic: Ambitions Enterprise Management rallies 129%
data.description:
Ambitions Enterprise Management (AHMA) rallied another 129% on Tuesday to extend its hot streak since going public in October. The Dubai-based company began trading on October 21 after pricing its IPO of 1.725 million shares at $4.00 per share. The stock has now tripled since then, with shares swapping hands as high as $12.38 earlier in the trading session. 

Ambitions Enterprise Management (AHMA) operates as a tour operator, travel agency, and provider of event planning and management services, primarily in the United Arab Emirates. The company organizes and executes a wide range of events, such as annual meetings, incentives, conferences, exhibitions, staff gatherings, product launches, and corporate hospitality. Revenue for the first six months of 2025 was just $9.7M. Looking ahead, AHMA aims to be a leading provider of multi-service business event management and travel products in the Middle East’s growing tourism and MICE sectors.

data.title: Delta signals 20% EPS growth and $3B–$4B free cash flow in 2026 as premium, loyalty, and international drive expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Delta Air Lines (DAL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ed Bastian, CEO, opened by stating Delta "delivered a strong close to our Centennial year with results that are a clear proof point of the differentiation and the durability that we built." He highlighted record revenue of $58.3 billion for the full year, an operating margin of 10%, pretax income of $5 billion, and earnings of $5.82 per share. Free cash flow for the year reached $4.6 billion, with leverage reduced by more than 50% over the past three years. Bastian emphasized that Delta's "return on invested capital of 12% is well above our cost of capital, placing us in the upper half of the S&P 500 and leading the industry."
  • Bastian announced a 4% pay increase and a $1.3 billion profit-sharing payout. He also introduced a new order for 30 Boeing 787-10s with options for 30 more, deliveries beginning in 2031, aimed at enhancing Delta's international network and long-haul capabilities. Bastian confirmed Glen Hauenstein's upcoming retirement and Joe Esposito's elevation to Chief Commercial Officer.
  • Glen W. Hauenstein, President, noted that premium revenue grew 7%, cargo increased 9%, maintenance repair and overhaul (MRO) revenue grew 25%, and loyalty revenue improved 6%. He stated, "diversified revenue streams now representing 60% of total revenue," and signaled high single-digit growth in co-brand remuneration for 2026, expecting to reach a $10 billion target within a few years.
  • Daniel Janki, CFO, stated, "In the fourth quarter, we delivered pretax profit of $1.3 billion, an operating margin of 10% and earnings of $1.55 per share." He highlighted that the government shutdown reduced pretax profit by $200 million or $0.25 per share. Janki also reported Delta ended the year with gross leverage of 2.4x, adjusted net debt of approximately $14 billion, and unencumbered assets of $35 billion.

Outlook

  • Delta projects revenue growth of 5% to 7% in the March quarter, with positive unit revenue growth. Janki stated, "we expect first quarter earnings of $0.50 to $0.90 per share and an operating margin of 4.5% to 6%, both improving year-over-year."
  • For full year 2026, Delta expects EPS of $6.50 to $7.50, representing 20% year-over-year growth at the midpoint, and free cash flow of $3 billion to $4 billion. Janki said, "leverage of 2x by year-end."
  • The company plans CapEx of $5.5 billion in 2026, including approximately 50 aircraft deliveries.

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, Delta reported pretax profit of $1.3 billion, an operating margin of 10%, and earnings of $1.55 per share. The company delivered record quarterly revenue of $14.6 billion, 1.2% higher than in 2024, on capacity growth of 1%.
  • Full-year results included record revenue of $58.3 billion and free cash flow of $4.6 billion. Delta reinvested $4.3 billion in the business and reduced debt by $2.6 billion.
  • Non-fuel CASM increased 4% year-over-year in the quarter. For the year, non-fuel unit cost growth was 2%.

Q&A

  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: Asked about the impact of a potential 10% rate cap on industry loyalty programs. Ed Bastian responded, "it's way early innings. And so it's really, really hard to speculate...we at Delta have the premium card in the industry."
  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: Questioned demand acceleration and main cabin fare movement. Hauenstein said, "the revenue has definitely accelerated here...right now, we're seeing both fare and traffic."
  • John Godyn, Citi: Queried corporate demand and guidance sensitivity. Hauenstein noted, "Delta's market share has never been higher...but it's a much broader than just Delta at this point." Bastian added, "if the momentum that we currently see continues will be fine, we'll do well on our guidance range."
  • Conor Cunningham, Melius: Asked about commercial strategy continuity. Joe Esposito indicated, "Our strategies aren't changing. In fact, I mean, we're going to be digging even deeper into those strategies for further integration."
  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James: Inquired about operational reliability post-COVID. Bastian acknowledged, "There's no question that we have work to do with respect to the resiliency of our recovery from irregular operations."
  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: Probed the premium vs. main cabin revenue spread. Hauenstein described, "the margin spreads, as you indicated, have never been greater."
  • Andrew Didora, BofA: Asked about free cash flow drivers. Janki explained, "the loyalty premium, certainly, given its margin profile, has an outsized impact on it."
  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: Sought details on international and domestic trends. Esposito reported, "sequential trends in international and domestic are both really positive as we go into 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about leadership transitions and robust financial performance, repeatedly congratulating Glen Hauenstein and probing growth sustainability and competitive positioning.
  • Management's prepared remarks were confident, using phrases such as "I have never been more confident of Delta's future." In Q&A, tone remained steady but included caution about volatility: "we want to make certain that we have a bit of caution as we project how we'll do."
  • Compared to last quarter, management's tone was similarly optimistic, with a slight increase in caution regarding industry volatility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 EPS was explicitly raised to $6.50 to $7.50, from the previous full-year guidance of approximately $6 per share for 2025.
  • Delta announced a new wide-body aircraft order and signaled further international expansion, compared to last quarter's focus on premium and loyalty expansion.
  • Both quarters highlighted strong premium and loyalty revenue, but the current quarter added more detail on diversification and cash generation.
  • Analysts continued to focus on demand trends, main cabin recovery, and competitive dynamics.
  • Management sentiment remained confident, with increased discussion of competitive differentiation and fleet renewal.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued industry volatility and the impact of government shutdowns, which reduced Q4 pretax profit by $200 million.
  • Bastian flagged the need for caution around potential regulatory changes impacting loyalty programs.
  • Operational recovery from irregular operations post-COVID remains a focus area for improvement.
  • Analyst questions highlighted uncertainty around main cabin demand and competitive responses to ongoing industry restructuring.

Final Takeaway

Delta Air Lines closed 2025 with record revenue and free cash flow, advanced its premium and loyalty strategies, and signaled robust growth for 2026, projecting 20% EPS growth and $3–$4 billion in free cash flow. Strategic investments in fleet renewal and international expansion, coupled with a strong balance sheet, position Delta for further margin growth. Management emphasized ongoing cost discipline, the strength of its premium and co-brand businesses, and a seamless transition in commercial leadership, underlining confidence in Delta's trajectory into the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Visa, Mastercard stocks slide after Trump targets swipe fees
data.description:
Visa (V) stock dropped 3.7% and Mastercard (MA) sank 3.7% in Tuesday afternoon trading after President Donald Trump rallied support for the Credit Card Competition Act, "in order to stop the out of control Swipe Fee ripoff."

The bill was originally introduced by Sen. Roger Marshall (R-KS) and Sen. Dick Durbin (D-IL) in the last Congress, and was introduced on Tuesday. "This legislation will increase competition in the credit card market and put an end to the Visa-Mastercard duopoly that is squeezing small businesses — and, ultimately, consumers," Marshall's statement said.

Trump's social media post on the CCCA followed his announcement late Friday that he would cap credit card interest fees at 10% for a year. On Tuesday, House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-LA) said Congress should consider the rate cap.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: MGM Resorts is downgraded at Truist as it waits out the Las Vegas recovery
data.description:
Truist Securities lowered its rating on MGM Resorts International (MGM) to Hold from Buy due mainly to concerns about trends in Las Vegas. 

"While we see MGM as a best-in-class diversified operator, we think shares tend to pivot to Strip performance and believe there could be downside to consensus estimates given continuing leisure consumer malaise appears to be more than offsetting group-driven upside for 2026. We do see a path for a return to growth later this year, but expect better or similar entry points," warned analyst Barry Jonas.

Notably, Jonas and his team expect 2026 Strip EBITDA to contract 1% year-over-year in 2026 as leisure malaise impacts the Las Vegas casino industry. The firm has no doubt that Las Vegas will bounce back to healthy growth but thinks it could take a while for a recovery to resonate with investors.

Truist clipped its price target on the casino operator to $38 from $45.

Shares of MGM Resorts (MGM) were down 1.5% in Tuesday afternoon action.

data.title: Concentrix outlines 2026 revenue target up to $10.18B with 3% growth focus amid AI expansion and margin improvement
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Concentrix (CNXC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Caldwell highlighted that "for the past few years, we have been clear about evolving our business to deliver more solutions that involve technology," emphasizing investments in AI and technology-enabled services. He noted a shift toward high-value, complex work, reporting that non-complex work was reduced from 7% to 5% of revenue, primarily through automation, and $95 million was invested in new capabilities and global footprint expansion.
  • Caldwell detailed the go-to-market overhaul, stating, "we retrained our entire sales and account management team, upgraded 25% of this team with enterprise sellers, invested in SME supporting technology solutions and developed a clear vertical offerings while building out our partner organization for a little over $25 million of incremental spend." This led to a 6% increase in annual contract value pipeline, 9% increase in new wins, 14% boost in transformational deal values, 23% rise in cross-sell/upsell deals, and a 37% jump in new service area values.
  • He pointed to the launch of the iX Suite AI platform, with over $60 million in annualized AI revenue and breakeven achieved. More than 40% of new business now includes proprietary technology. Caldwell said, "this attach rate is well ahead of our expectations."
  • Caldwell described operational efficiencies, mentioning a reduction of close to $100 million in expenditures on non-billable resources and infrastructure by Q1 2026's exit, redirecting these funds into growth areas.
  • Key client wins were highlighted, such as a major global bank, an electric car company, and an AI model maker, illustrating the company's growing reputation as a trusted end-to-end solutions partner.
  • CFO Andre Valentine stated, "we accelerated revenue growth in each sequential quarter. We achieved breakeven profitability with our iX suite. We generated record adjusted free cash flow, growing our adjusted free cash flow by over $150 million from the prior year. We returned a record $258 million to shareholders... and we reduced our net debt."

Outlook

  • For 2026, Valentine gave guidance for full-year reported revenue of $10.035 billion to $10.180 billion, implying constant currency revenue growth of 1.5% to 3% and a 60 basis point positive impact from foreign exchange rates. Non-GAAP operating income is expected between $1.24 billion to $1.29 billion, and non-GAAP EPS is projected at $11.48 to $12.07 per share.
  • Valentine highlighted expectations for ongoing cost efficiencies and investments in technology and go-to-market capabilities, with sequential quarterly increases in non-GAAP operating income anticipated in the second half of 2026 as duplicate costs are removed.
  • Adjusted free cash flow for 2026 is expected in the $630 million to $650 million range.
  • Q1 2026 revenue is expected at $2.475 billion to $2.50 billion, with non-GAAP operating income of $290 million to $300 million and non-GAAP EPS of $2.57 to $2.69 per share.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 revenue was reported at $2.55 billion, representing 3.1% constant currency growth, above prior guidance. By vertical, banking, financial services and insurance grew 11%, communications and media 7%, travel 7%, and other (including automotive) 7%. Technology/consumer electronics and health care declined by approximately 2% each.
  • Non-GAAP operating income was $323 million, with a non-GAAP operating margin of 12.7%, a sequential increase of 40 basis points. Adjusted EBITDA reached $379 million (14.8% margin), while non-GAAP net income was $192 million, and non-GAAP diluted EPS was $2.95.
  • Adjusted free cash flow in the quarter was over $287 million, a quarterly record. The company returned nearly $80 million to shareholders in Q4 through dividends and share repurchases.
  • A $1.52 billion noncash goodwill impairment charge was recorded, reflecting stock trading range.
  • Fiscal year 2025 revenue grew 2.1% in constant currency, with non-GAAP operating income of $1.254 billion and adjusted free cash flow of $626 million.

Q&A

  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA Securities, asked about AI investment metrics and CapEx/OpEx strategy. Caldwell responded, "when we look at our metrics on our AI, our pure own AI platform, we were very committed to making sure that we could be accretive this year and hit a certain revenue goal... we achieved that... with $60 million of run rate on sort of a total spend of around a little over $50 million."
  • Bhattacharya further asked about supporting clients facing slowdowns and upfront investment. Caldwell replied that investment decisions are based on client type and long-term potential, not call volumes, with a preference for "long-term value-focused client[s]" and nearly 18 years of service among top 25 clients.
  • Bhattacharya inquired about market growth rates and Webhelp synergies. Caldwell stated, "Webhelp absolutely met our expectations, if not a little better... we met our synergy goals, in fact, just slightly exceeded them from a cost takeout perspective."
  • David Koning, Baird, asked about margin trajectory. Valentine stated, "we expect to see sequential improvement in the back half of this year in margins as we complete working through some of the overcapacity issues of the tariffs..."
  • Lucas Morison, Canaccord Genuity, asked about growth drags from low-complexity/offshore transitions. Caldwell said, "we did 2% in '25. We expect 1% in '26. We always expect there will be some portion of low complexity work as part of our portfolio."
  • Vincent Colicchio, Barrington Research, asked about consolidation and talent acquisition. Caldwell confirmed continued expectations for consolidation and said, "we have a global footprint that we can pull from, and that's been very, very helpful to us."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of AI investments, margin improvement, and growth headwinds, with a tone that was largely neutral but probing for underlying risks.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, frequently referencing achievements and reiterating guidance. Caldwell and Valentine consistently described results as "pleasing" and prospects as "exciting," while using phrases such as "we are confident" and "we're excited about the road ahead."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management tone remained optimistic, with slightly increased emphasis on cost discipline and margin recovery. Analysts' tone was consistent, remaining neutral to slightly cautious on margin and growth sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is now explicit, with revenue of $10.035 billion to $10.180 billion vs. prior guidance for 2025 of $9.798 billion to $9.823 billion.
  • Management's commentary shifted from discussing margin recovery as a future goal (Q3) to highlighting sequential improvements and specific actions already taken (Q4).
  • The Q4 update included detailed metrics on AI platform performance and investment returns, expanding on the prior quarter’s more general statements about technology differentiation.
  • Analysts continued to focus on AI, margins, and growth headwinds, with recurring questions about transformation investments and market share.
  • Management’s confidence in cost reduction and operational efficiency was more pronounced in Q4, with CEO Caldwell emphasizing, "we have been very disciplined in driving efficiencies in our own business so we can invest in the areas we have just talked about."

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued revenue headwinds from proactive reduction in non-complex work (1% impact in 2026) and further resolutioning of client programs (2% impact in 2026).
  • Margin pressure remains due to ongoing investments, duplicate costs during transitions, and the evolving mix of business. Valentine noted the expectation to remove duplicate costs and simplify the business during 2026.
  • Analysts questioned the longer-term impact of low-complexity runoff and the sustainability of current margin improvement trends.

Final Takeaway

Concentrix closed 2025 with strong momentum in technology-enabled services, achieving record free cash flow and significant AI platform growth. Management projects continued revenue and margin expansion into 2026, supported by disciplined investment, operational efficiencies, and a growing reputation for complex, high-value client solutions. The company remains confident in its ability to capture market share, scale new service offerings, and deliver shareholder returns, despite anticipated headwinds from business mix shifts and continued investment in transformation initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citi analysts express confidence that the Fed's independence will hold
data.description:
Analysts at Citi Research on Tuesday said the very structure of the Federal Reserve preserves its independence and that markets would have acted as a "correcting mechanism" if the central bank's independence really was an issue. 

The comments were issued in a virtual session at a Citi Research conference. They come just days after the Trump administration launched a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who publicly said the probe was not about legitimate legal concerns but a way to punish his refusal to set interest rates according to the president's wishes.

"I am not as worried about challenges to central bank independence as some others, and that's partly to do with the structure of the central bank itself," Andrew Hollenhorst, chief U.S. economist at Citi Research, said in the session. 

According to the economist, the three-pronged structure of the Fed, consisting of a board of seven governors, 12 regional reserve banks, and a monetary policy committee, insulates it against political interference.

"I want to give the framers of the Federal Reserve Act a little bit of credit here," Hollenhorst said, adding, "We emerged with an organization that has a lot of features to it that insulate the Federal Reserve."

"So if you look at those governors, these are governors who are appointed by the president, and the term for a governor is 14 years, so this is meant to span across administrations; really, you could say across political cycles, a lot of changes in politics in 14 years. So that's one way that the central bank preserves its independence," he said.

Meanwhile, Jason Williams, senior U.S. rates strategist at Citi Research, said that the bond market's reaction to the news on Monday was not representative of alarm. 

"We didn't rally, we kind of rallied overnight in some of the SOFR futures, but by the time New York trading was open, there was no repricing in the front-end as though now Trump can bully the Fed to deliver material rate cuts," the strategist said in the session.

"It was interesting how gold was really the one trade that did hold the gains in this independence narrative," Williams noted.

Robert Sockin, senior global economist at Citi Research, highlighted the market's role in acting as a "correcting mechanism."

"(If) the market was really sensing a real issue here, you would see probably the long-end move up significantly, inflation expectations move," Sockin said.

"That would maybe hold the hand of any administration that was looking to have (undue) influence on the Fed," he added.

"I am concerned, but I think central bank independence will hold," the economist said. 

"The Fed is filled with thousands of technocratic staff members. If there was a real issue of politicizing the Fed, I would expect we'd see things like resignations across the Fed, maybe some of them quite senior. Haven't seen anything like that, and that's another thing that leads me to believe that the institution will hold," Sockin added.

U.S. stocks were largely lower on Tuesday, as market participants continued to digest the Fed news and the latest reading on consumer inflation. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).
